story:
Summer on our balcony tasted like salty manakish and smelled like olives and sun. Cheese grease puddled on our plates while the valley shone green—figs, jujubes, and Mounir’s chickens clucking over their breakfast. Mom set down tea and dropped her news like a stone in a quiet pond: Malek’s cousin Lulu was visiting from France to get a good impression of us and the village. Next year, Malek would move there. One day, they’d get engaged. My slingshot hand went cold.

I hunted Malek down on the front steps. He said he hadn’t told me because I’d be mad. He was right. Beirut’s fighting had pushed his family to our mountain village already, and now he was going farther—forever. He said France meant safety, school, and a shot at becoming a writer in French. I said home was here, war or not. We were twelve—too young for rings and forever. In frustration, I smeared cheese grease across his cheek. It didn’t change a thing.

Lulu arrived with her parents, all French words and bright eyes. Auntie Mia asked her to speak English so we could all talk. Mom warned me not to drag Lulu into our rough games. I promised nothing and gathered Dani and Rami, our twin cousins, and led everyone down the white stone steps to Dad’s gardens.

I told Lulu that if she wanted to hang with us, she had to pass our tests: free Mounir’s chickens, put them back, then “borrow” a branch from old man Hameed’s best jujube tree. Malek pulled me aside—no thief roles for Lulu, please. He wanted her to make a good impression. I agreed with my mouth and broke the deal with my eyes.

We slipped into Mounir’s farm. Lulu hugged her skirt to her legs, but when I showed her the latch, she popped it. Buck-buck-buck—pandemonium. I chased “the thief,” tapped her lightly, and tackled her into the grass. She surprised us both by laughing, loud and bubbly, even with mud on her clothes. Then Mounir’s voice stabbed the air. We ran, hearts banging. Lulu’s outfit was ruined; she grinned anyway and grabbed Malek’s hand. My jaw clenched.

We climbed to Hameed’s yard. The twins set a ladder. Lulu went up. Leaves rained like green confetti. Malek steadied the rungs, his face flushed. Jealous, I slipped my slingshot to the twins to “test” the thief. A pebble zipped past her cheek; she flinched, yanked too hard, and tumbled. No real harm done—just grass stains and a shaken breath—but Malek’s anger was sharp. I had lied. I had pushed too far. We snapped at each other: boys versus men, home versus change. He said some of us had to grow. I said I wouldn’t abandon the place that made me.

Then Hameed burst out, belt in hand. He came for me. Malek stepped between us. Lulu wrapped Malek in a hug, blocking the swing. The old man huffed back inside, promising to tell my dad. Our fear cooled into a warm buzz. I saw the fallen jujube branch and lifted it like a trophy. We cheered for the best thief we ever had. We knew trespassing and taking fruit was wrong; the scare taught us as much.

After lunch, it was goodbye time. Malek went down to the road with his father to tie the bags onto the car, and on the balcony, Lulu admitted she had two homes now: France, and this valley she barely knew but felt anyway. I confessed my jealousy and said I wanted Malek happy—even in France, writing in French about this place, maybe about me. She hoped they’d come back one day. I said that would be great.

Malek came back up from the road, bumped my shoulder, and said, “So you were jealous after all.”

Down in the distance, Mounir shook his fist at the sky and shouted my name. I laughed. Maybe everything changes. Maybe not everything.
